title: Infinite Series (Comparison V2)
description: Module 2 — theorem spine and convergence test strategy

edges.label: a series \(\sum a_n\) is built by formally \_\_\_ all terms of \(\{a_n\}\)
edges.hint: The Σ symbol indicates this operation.

edges.label: for \(s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k\), the object \(s_n\) is the \_\_\_ sum
edges.hint: You stop at a finite cutoff index \(n\), not at infinity.

edges.label: definition-level: \(\sum a_n\) converges iff \(\{s_n\}\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Convergence of a series is defined by the convergence target of \(s_n\).

edges.label: implication check: \(\sum a_n\) diverges iff \(\{s_n\}\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Use exact negation of the convergence definition for \(s_n\).

edges.label: contrapositive use: if \(a_n \not\to 0\), then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: If a necessary condition fails, the theorem conclusion cannot hold.

edges.label: Cauchy criterion (series tails): for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(N\) such that \(|a_{m+1}+\cdots+a_n|\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The key is uniform control of every sufficiently far tail, not one specific tail.

edges.label: \(\sum ar^n\) converges precisely when \(|r|\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: Strict inequality matters here.

edges.label: harmonic proof idea: grouping terms in blocks shows partial sums grow without bound, so they are \_\_\_
edges.hint: Each dyadic block contributes at least a fixed positive amount.

edges.label: \(\sum 1/n^p\) converges \(\iff\) \(p\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: Harmonic (\(p=1\)) is the boundary case — diverges.

edges.label: proof step: if \(a_n\ge 0\) and partial sums are bounded above, then \(\{s_n\}\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Use monotone convergence theorem on partial sums of nonnegative terms.

edges.label: integral-test proof step: compare rectangles with area under \(f\), so \(f\) should be eventually \_\_\_
edges.hint: Monotone decrease is what makes left/right rectangle bounds work.

edges.label: \(\int_1^\infty x^{-p}\,dx\) converges precisely when \(p\) \_\_\_ \(1\)
edges.hint: The improper integral is finite iff the exponent makes the antiderivative tend to a constant.

edges.label: direct comparison (eventual nonnegative order): if \(0\le b_n\le a_n\) for all large \(n\), and \(\sum b_n\) diverges, then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: The larger nonnegative tail cannot converge if a smaller benchmark already diverges.

edges.label: limit comparison: if \(\lim a_n/b_n = L \in (0, \infty)\), then the two series \_\_\_
edges.hint: A finite positive comparison limit means asymptotically comparable size.

edges.label: ratio test: if \(L=\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|<1\), then \(\sum a_n\) converges \_\_\_
edges.hint: Compare eventually to a geometric series with ratio strictly below 1.

edges.label: root test: if \(\limsup |a_n|^{1/n} < 1\), then \(\sum a_n\) converges \_\_\_
edges.hint: The root test compares term magnitudes to a geometric series.

edges.label: a \((-1)^n\) factor makes the signs \_\_\_ each term
edges.hint: \(+, -, +, -, \ldots\) — the signs ___ at each step.

edges.label: Leibniz precision: it is enough that \(b_n\) is eventually decreasing and \(b_n \to\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: A finite number of early exceptions does not affect convergence.

edges.label: logic check: ordinary convergence always implies absolute convergence
edges.hint: Use the alternating harmonic series as the canonical counterexample.

edges.label: test-setup step: absolute convergence means apply tests to \(\sum\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Replace each term by its magnitude before applying ratio/root/comparison tools.

edges.label: a series that converges but \(\sum |a_n|\) does NOT — is called \_\_\_ convergent
edges.hint: The convergence depends on cancellation between positive and negative terms.

edges.label: Leibniz remainder fact: if \(S\) is the sum, then \(|S-s_n|\le\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Error magnitude is controlled by the first omitted alternating-term magnitude.

edges.label: ratio boundary case: when \(L=\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|=1\), the ratio test is \_\_\_
edges.hint: Classic counterexamples at \(L=1\): \(\sum 1/n\) vs \(\sum 1/n^2\).

edges.label: root test checkpoint: with \(C=\limsup |a_n|^{1/n}\), the only always-true statement is that \(C=1\) is \_\_\_
options: equivalent to ratio test with L<1
edges.hint: At the boundary value, root and ratio tests do not decide by themselves.

edges.label: Dirichlet step: if \(B_n=\sum_{k=1}^n b_k\) is bounded and \(a_n\downarrow 0\), then \(\sum a_n b_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Bounded partial sums plus monotone-to-zero coefficient is the core pairing.

edges.label: strategy with concrete form: for \(a_n=\frac{n}{n^2+3}\), ratio/root are boundary-inconclusive; compare with \(1/n\) using \_\_\_
edges.hint: At boundary-inconclusive cases, switch to an asymptotic benchmark test.

edges.label: test-selection: for \(a_n=\frac{n!}{3^n}\), the most direct first test is the \_\_\_ test
edges.hint: Factorials/exponentials are typically handled quickest by consecutive-term ratios.

edges.label: integral test diagnosis: if \(f(n)=a_n>0\) but \(f\) is not eventually decreasing, the integral test is typically \_\_\_
edges.hint: Missing key hypotheses means the theorem cannot be safely applied.

edges.label: logic check: \(a_n\to 0\) is a \_\_\_ condition for \(\sum a_n\) to converge
edges.hint: Harmonic gives the standard counterexample to sufficiency.

edges.label: error target: for an alternating series with \(b_n=1/n\), to guarantee \(|S-s_n|<0.01\), it is enough to choose \(n\) so that \_\_\_
edges.hint: Leibniz remainder is bounded by the first omitted magnitude \(b_{n+1}\).

edges.label: strategy step: if ratio/root gives boundary value \(1\) for \(a_n=1/n^p\), the strongest next test among these is \_\_\_
edges.hint: For positive monotone reciprocal-power forms, integral test gives the threshold \(p>1\).

edges.label: method distinction: proving \(\sum 1\) diverges because terms do not go to 0 uses the \_\_\_
edges.hint: This conclusion comes before any comparison: necessary condition fails immediately.

edges.label: one-sided limit comparison: if \(a_n,b_n\ge 0\), \(\limsup a_n/b_n<\infty\), and \(\sum b_n\) converges, then \(\sum a_n\) \_\_\_
edges.hint: Finite asymptotic upper ratio makes \(a_n\) eventually bounded by a constant multiple of \(b_n\).

edges.label: diagnose misuse: if \(\lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|\) does NOT exist, saying "ratio test proves convergence" is generally \_\_\_
options: equivalent to root test with C<1
edges.hint: No limit means the standard ratio criterion does not directly decide.

edges.label: capstone workflow: for \(\sum (-1)^n n/2^n\), the strongest shortest route is to test \(\sum |a_n|\) by ratio/root, conclude \_\_\_
edges.hint: Exponential denominator dominates polynomial numerator, so magnitude series converges.
